text:
GASTROINTESTINAL ONCOLOGY — OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [GI Oncology Attending]
SERVICE: Medical Oncology — Gastrointestinal Malignancies
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, treatment monitoring visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski, a 67-year-old male with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), presents for oncology follow-up and assessment after completing cycle 4 of 6 planned cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine (APACT regimen). This is a routine treatment monitoring and toxicity evaluation visit.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is a retired pipe fitter with a well-characterized history of borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53 p.R248W, SMAD4 deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion; microsatellite stable, HRD-negative) who has now completed cycle 4 of 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. His treatment history to date has been extensive and is summarized below.

The patient was initially diagnosed with a 3.8 cm borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, Stage III) approximately 16 months ago, with CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1,200 U/mL and biliary obstruction requiring ERCP with biliary stenting. Following multidisciplinary tumor board review, he underwent neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles, demonstrating an excellent response with CA 19-9 declining to 85 U/mL and tumor shrinkage from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with improved vascular geometry around the SMA and SMV. This was followed by consolidative long-course chemoradiation — IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine — after which repeat pancreatic protocol CT showed further reduction to 2.1 cm with largely restored SMA fat plane and a patent SMV-portal vein confluence.

He subsequently underwent open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental SMV resection and end-to-end reconstruction approximately 7 months into the disease course. Surgical pathology confirmed residual moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma measuring 2.0 cm with CAP treatment response grade 2, and an R0 resection was achieved at all margins. Three of 18 lymph nodes were positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB). Perineural and lymphovascular invasion were present.

The postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPS criteria), which required readmission, IV antibiotics, TPN, octreotide, and CT-guided drain repositioning. This complication resolved over approximately 5 to 6 weeks and delayed initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy to approximately 10 weeks postoperatively. Once cleared by surgery, he was started on gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m² IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle, plus capecitabine 830 mg/m² PO BID on days 1 through 21 (dose-reduced from 1,660 mg/m²/day due to grade 1 hand-foot syndrome that developed in cycle 2). Gemcitabine day 15 was held in cycle 2 due to grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 88 x 10⁹/L). Through cycles 1-3, he experienced manageable but notable toxicity including grade 2 fatigue, mild nausea, grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, and stable grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy in the bilateral lower extremities (residual from prior neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX).

A surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 3 months after surgery (mid-adjuvant therapy, between cycles 3 and 4) demonstrated no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease, with a CA 19-9 of 18 U/mL. The patient tolerated cycles 3 and 4 without major incident.

Today, he presents approximately 4 weeks after completing cycle 4. He reports manageable fatigue, which he describes as mild-to-moderate and primarily manifesting as afternoon energy decline. He has been able to maintain light activities around the home. He continues to note a sensation of tingling and numbness in his toes and soles of both feet, consistent with his known residual oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, and feels this has been stable rather than worsening. His hands are improved compared to earlier in his FOLFIRINOX course. He denies significant hand-foot syndrome symptoms at this time and reports no blistering, fissuring, or painful redness of the palms or soles. He reports occasional mild nausea around the time he takes oral capecitabine, which is controlled with scheduled ondansetron. He denies diarrhea, constipation, mucositis, or dysphagia. He is tolerating a regular diet with modifications for his pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. He is taking his Creon (pancrelipase) with meals and snacks and reports that steatorrhea and bloating have been well controlled on his current dose. He is managing his blood sugars with basal-bolus insulin (glargine plus aspart) under endocrinology co-management and reports generally acceptable glucose readings, though he continues to struggle with postprandial spikes.

He started duloxetine 30 mg daily approximately 4 weeks ago, per neurology recommendation, for symptomatic management of peripheral neuropathy. He reports mild improvement in his neuropathic symptoms since initiation and no significant side effects from the duloxetine. He denies depressed mood or anxiety.

He has no new complaints referrable to the surgical site, no right upper quadrant pain, no back pain, no jaundice, and no new abdominal symptoms. He denies weight loss in recent weeks; in fact, he notes his weight has been gradually creeping back up.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild-to-moderate fatigue. Appetite fair to good. Weight trending upward (per patient report). No fevers, chills, or night sweats.
- Gastrointestinal: Occasional mild nausea with oral cape, controlled with ondansetron PRN. No vomiting. No diarrhea. No mucositis. No dysphagia. Steatorrhea well controlled with Creon. No abdominal pain. No early satiety beyond expected post-Whipple anatomy.
- Neurological: Bilateral lower extremity numbness and tingling in toes and soles (stable, grade 1-2). Mild improvement on duloxetine. No new upper extremity symptoms. No balance impairment.
- Dermatological: No active hand-foot syndrome. Mild residual dryness of palms, managed with emollient cream.
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising, bleeding, or infection symptoms. No febrile episodes since resolution of pancreatic fistula.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Labile blood sugars, working with endocrinology. On insulin.
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria or dysuria.
- Psychological: No depressed mood. Positive affect. Patient and wife express gratitude for how things have progressed.

---

PAST MEDICAL/ONCOLOGIC HISTORY:
- Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head of pancreas, initially cT3N1M0 (borderline resectable, AJCC 8th ed.) — now surgically resected, ypT2N1M0, R0, post-neoadjuvant multimodality therapy
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus, now insulin-dependent post-pancreatectomy (basal-bolus regimen)
- Hypertension
- Chronic peripheral sensory neuropathy (residual, bilateral lower extremities > upper extremities) — grade 1-2, secondary to prior oxaliplatin
- Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (post-Whipple) — on enzyme replacement therapy
- Prior biliary stenting (plastic stent, ERCP) — stent removed prior to surgery
- Prior Grade B pancreatic fistula post-Whipple — resolved

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental SMV resection and end-to-end reconstruction — approximately 7 months ago

ALLERGIES: NKDA

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired pipe fitter
- Former smoker: 30 pack-year history, quit approximately 15 years ago
- Lives with wife; supportive home environment
- No current alcohol use
- No illicit drug use

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No family history of pancreatic cancer
- Father with coronary artery disease

MEDICATIONS:
1. Gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15 of each 28-day cycle (adjuvant, cycle 4 of 6 completed)
2. Capecitabine (Xeloda) 750 mg/m² PO BID days 1-21 (dose-reduced from 830 mg/m² BID; adjuvant)
3. Insulin glargine (Lantus) — dose per endocrinology
4. Insulin aspart (NovoLog) — sliding scale, with meals
5. Metformin — currently HELD (held during active chemotherapy per institutional protocol; to be reassessed)
6. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (hypertension)
7. Pancrelipase (Creon 36,000 units) with meals and snacks
8. Duloxetine (Cymbalta) 30 mg PO daily (peripheral neuropathy, initiated approximately 4 weeks ago)
9. Ondansetron 4 mg PO PRN nausea (every 6 hours as needed)
10. Emollient cream (Udderly Smooth or equivalent) to hands/feet BID
11. Multivitamin daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Engaged and conversational. Slightly fatigued appearing but comfortable.

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.2°F (36.8°C), oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight today: [documented by nursing staff]. Patient reports weight is modestly improved from his nadir following the pancreatic fistula complication.

HEENT: Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae non-pale. Oropharynx clear, mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, crackles, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Well-healed midline abdominal incision without erythema, induration, or dehiscence. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No palpable mass in the surgical bed. Normal bowel sounds. No ascites.

Extremities: No clubbing. No cyanosis. Bilateral lower extremity: no edema. Skin intact on hands and feet — no active blistering, fissuring, or erythema consistent with hand-foot syndrome; mild residual xerosis of palms.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities. Decreased light touch sensation in the bilateral distal lower extremities (toes and soles) bilaterally, consistent with known grade 1-2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. Proprioception mildly reduced at the level of the toes bilaterally. Gait steady; no ataxia. Deep tendon reflexes mildly reduced at the Achilles bilaterally.

Skin: No rash. No jaundice. Mild pallor consistent with chronic illness and chemotherapy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.1 x 10⁹/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable)
- Platelets: 142 x 10⁹/L (adequate; improved from nadir of 88 in cycle 2)
- ANC: 2.4 x 10⁹/L (adequate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL (baseline)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- AST: 28 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 38 U/L (mild, within acceptable range; previously 52 U/L cycle 2 — trending down)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
- Glucose (fasting): 138 mg/dL (elevated; known diabetic, endocrinology managing)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (within normal limits; stable from prior value of 18 U/mL; reassuringly normal)
- CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (normal)

HbA1c: 7.9% (improved from earlier value; still above goal — endocrinology co-managing)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (performed approximately 3 months after Whipple, between cycles 3 and 4): No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the surgical bed. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No new lymphadenopathy. Post-Whipple anatomy with expected postsurgical changes. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. The next surveillance CT is planned following completion of all 6 adjuvant cycles.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Kowalski is a 67-year-old male with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4, CDKN2A; MSS; HRD-negative) who has undergone definitive multimodality treatment — neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, long-course chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine, open Whipple procedure with R0 resection and segmental SMV reconstruction (ypT2N1M0), and now is on cycle 4 of 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine per the APACT regimen. He is currently with no evidence of disease (NED) and is tolerating adjuvant therapy reasonably well.

1. PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — NED, on adjuvant therapy:
   - Patient has completed cycle 4 of 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. CA 19-9 remains normal at 18 U/mL, consistent with no biochemical evidence of recurrence. Most recent surveillance imaging (approximately 6 weeks ago) showed no recurrence. The oncologic picture is reassuring.
   - Plan: Proceed with cycle 5 of 6 adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine today. Gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m² IV days 1, 8, 15. Capecitabine 750 mg/m² PO BID days 1-21 (maintained at dose-reduced level given prior HFS and tolerability). Antiemetics per standard protocol. Pegfilgrastim is not routinely indicated with this regimen; hold in reserve for significant neutropenia if it develops.
   - Pre-cycle labs reviewed and acceptable: ANC 2.4 x 10⁹/L, platelets 142 x 10⁹/L, bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL. Proceed with full gemcitabine dose.
   - Following completion of cycle 6, plan to transition to active surveillance per NCCN guidelines: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3-4 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months for years 3-5; CA 19-9 every 3 months.

2. PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY (grade 1-2, bilateral lower extremities, oxaliplatin-related — residual):
   - Duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated approximately 4 weeks ago per neurology recommendation and the patient reports mild but noticeable symptomatic improvement. This is encouraging.
   - Continue duloxetine at current dose. Neurology to consider uptitration to 60 mg daily if plateau of benefit achieved without side effects.
   - Patient counseled that residual neuropathy from cumulative oxaliplatin exposure may persist indefinitely, though some improvement over months to years is possible. Close monitoring will continue.
   - No current dose modification of gemcitabine/capecitabine indicated for neuropathy, as this toxicity is attributable to prior oxaliplatin rather than the current regimen.

3. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (grade 1, capecitabine-related, currently quiescent):
   - Grade 1 HFS resolved with capecitabine dose reduction to 750 mg/m² BID in cycle 2. No active HFS today — palms/soles examined and clear.
   - Continue emollient cream prophylactically BID. Patient educated to notify clinic immediately if blistering, cracking, or painful erythema recurs.
   - Maintain current capecitabine dose reduction. No escalation planned.

4. INSULIN-DEPENDENT DIABETES MELLITUS (post-pancreatectomy):
   - HbA1c 7.9%, improved from earlier in the course. Endocrinology co-managing with basal-bolus insulin regimen. Glucose today 138 mg/dL (fasting). Ongoing optimization is needed.
   - Metformin remains held during active chemotherapy. Reassess upon completion of adjuvant therapy and in context of renal function.
   - Glycemic management will be particularly important as we approach the post-treatment surveillance phase; tight control may reduce cancer recurrence risk and improve overall health outcomes.

5. PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY:
   - Well controlled on Creon 36,000 units per meal. Patient reports no active steatorrhea or bloating. Continue current dosing. Patient reminded to take enzymes with all meals and snacks. Nutritional status appears to be slowly improving.

6. FATIGUE (grade 2):
   - Multi-factorial in etiology: ongoing chemotherapy, post-major surgery recovery, anemia (Hgb 11.8), diabetes, and deconditioning. Hemoglobin stable; no indication for erythropoiesis-stimulating agents at this level.
   - Counseled on energy conservation techniques. Encouraged to engage in light aerobic activity (walking 15-20 minutes daily as tolerated) which has evidence-based benefit for cancer-related fatigue.
   - Reassure patient that fatigue is expected to improve substantially following completion of adjuvant therapy.

7. HYPERTENSION:
   - Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg today — well controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. Continue without change.

8. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING:
   - Following completion of cycle 6, the first post-adjuvant surveillance CT (chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast) will be planned. CA 19-9 at that time will also serve as a new baseline for the surveillance phase.
   - Patient and spouse engaged and well-informed. They have been through an arduous treatment course and are performing admirably. We discussed the statistical context: that while ypT2N1M0 disease with positive nodes confers a meaningful recurrence risk (particularly in the first 2 years), the combination of an R0 resection, excellent biochemical response (CA 19-9 normalized from 1,200 to current 18 U/mL), and completion of full adjuvant therapy is a favorable constellation of findings.
   - The patient was encouraged to contact the clinic with any new or worsening symptoms, particularly right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, new back pain, or unintentional weight loss.

Return to clinic in approximately 4 weeks for cycle 6 initiation and assessment, or sooner if clinically indicated.

Total face-to-face time with patient and spouse: approximately 45 minutes. Medical decision-making: high complexity given active cancer treatment, multiple comorbidities, ongoing toxicity management, and treatment planning.

[Electronically signed — GI Oncology Attending]
